items.title: Report center and spread together
items.avoid: Publishing only E[X] or only the sample mean without any measure of spread.
items.prefer: Pair mean with standard deviation, variance, or quantiles so stakeholders see both typical value and uncertainty.
items.why: Two systems with the same average latency can have very different tail risk; variance carries that information.
items.example: Report 'mean latency 120 ms, σ = 40 ms' rather than 'mean latency 120 ms' alone when discussing SLAs.

items.title: Use the right variance formula for the job
items.avoid: Mixing up biased (divisor n) and unbiased (divisor n−1) variance when comparing to textbook Var(X) or sklearn defaults.
items.prefer: Use `np.var(x, ddof=1)` or `pandas.Series.var()` for sample variance; use divisor n only when defining training MSE over a fixed set.
items.why: Inconsistent divisors make hand calculations disagree with library output and inflate or deflate reported spread.
items.example: For sklearn `mean_squared_error(y, y_pred)` on n points, the value is (1/n)Σ(y−ŷ)²—population-style MSE, not s² with n−1.

items.title: Check linearity scope before simplifying
items.avoid: Extending E[X + Y] = E[X] + E[Y] to Var(X + Y) or E[XY] without covariance or dependence analysis.
items.prefer: Apply linearity to expectations of sums; for variance of sums, add 2 Cov(X, Y); for products, use E[XY] = Cov(X, Y) + E[X]E[Y].
items.why: Over-applying linearity is a top source of wrong risk aggregation and feature engineering errors.
items.example: Summing two highly correlated error terms requires Cov in Var(error1 + error2), not just Var(error1) + Var(error2).
